bias: Left‑leaning coverage emphasizes human cost and escalation—foregrounding words like "Seventh" and "killed" and framing events as an "Iran war ," plus use of "retaliatory strikes"—while right‑leaning pieces spotlight national‑security framing and ceremonial patriotism, invoking "Operation Epic Fury," "dignified transfer," and phrases like "Hardest Thing I Have To Do" to evoke honor and resolve; center reporting is more source‑attributed and highlights civilian infrastructure damage and identification details.

Pivotal divides include Dover/presidential attendance, civilian‑infrastructure reporting, and an unverified Iranian succession claim; all sides nonetheless echo CENTCOM’s account, the death, and the tally of seven.
summary: On March 8, 2026, U.S. Central Command announced a U.S. service member died from wounds sustained in an attack on U.S. troops in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia on March 1, 2026.
Following the Feb. 28 strikes on Iran, Iran responded with drone and missile strikes targeting Gulf countries and U.S. bases, including Prince Sultan Air Base.
The military is withholding the service member's identity until 24 hours after next-of-kin notification, and six Army Reserve members were killed in a March 1 drone strike in Kuwait.
The Pentagon said the death raises U.S. military fatalities to seven, and President Donald Trump attended a Saturday dignified transfer at Dover Air Force Base, Delaware.

Humanitarian agencies report the conflict has damaged civilian infrastructure, with the Iranian Red Crescent Society citing about 10,000 damaged structures and Lebanon reporting over a half-million displaced, while Bahrain accused Iran of striking a desalination plant.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes instability and judgment, framing the exit with terms like "controversial," "tumultuous tenure," and "for a second time," portraying the departure as evidence of broader trouble under the administration; right-leaning pieces de-emphasize policy detail and instead foreground the announcement and labels like "embattled" and references to " Trump administration " to personalize the drama.

Center coverage sticks closer to procedural background—ouster/reinstatement and specific vaccine or rare-disease policy disputes—while both sides agree on the April departure and that Prasad’s tenure drew contention, reflecting deeper ideological splits over regulatory politics.
summary: The FDA confirmed Vinay Prasad, a top official overseeing vaccines and gene therapies, will depart in April and has not named a successor.

On 2026-03-06, the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research increased scrutiny and rejected at least five cell and gene therapies, with a recent high-profile rejection of a rare-blood-cancer drug, amid disputes with drugmakers, and the Health and Human Services Department criticized an experimental Huntington's treatment the day before.
Sources describe management actions that reshaped CBER's internal operations, including firing staff and demanding calendar visibility from California, after a brief ouster and rehiring in July.

Prasad's actions produced immediate regulatory consequences, including a White House-intervened review after he refused to review a Moderna flu vaccine, and control of vaccine surveillance shifted from career staff.

Amid a broader shift under Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Prasad's exit signals wider policy implications as the FDA adopts a more vaccine-skeptical approach; this is a developing story STAT will update.
title: FDA vaccines chief Vinay Prasad to step down in April

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes Russian culpability and escalation, framing Moscow as "aiding" Iran and using phrases like "help Tehran strike" and "comprehensive effort"; right-leaning pieces stress security threat, invoke "attack" and "dragging a nuclear rival into the war," and name "American warships" and " aircraft " to raise urgency.

The left uses accusatory language while the right deploys militarized, alarmist diction; dividing lines include Lavrov's rhetoric, center caveats about intelligence limits and White House "downplayed" response, and technical details .
Both sides agree U.S. officials reported shared targeting data, revealing a split between security and accountability priorities.
summary: On Mar 6, 2026, two U.S. officials told the AP that Russia provided Iran with information that could help Tehran strike American warships and aircraft.
Moscow tightened ties as it sought missiles and drones for its war in Ukraine, while Tehran supplied Shahed drones used in retaliatory Gulf attacks.
Space-Based tracking showed Moscow's about 110 military satellites and Iran's around 13 enable imagery and target monitoring of the USS Gerald R. Ford.

The White House downplayed reports of Russia sharing intelligence with Iran, while US Central Command's Admiral Brad Cooper said `This is unacceptable and will not go unanswered` amid Iranian drone and missile strikes in Bahrain.
Analysts warn the conflict has slowed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and raised oil prices, while the UN refugee agency calls the situation a major humanitarian emergency.
title: Russia Sharing Intelligence with Iran to Target US Forces, Officials Say

summary: U.S. President Donald Trump stated that the only acceptable outcome for ending hostilities with Iran is 'unconditional surrender.'
Trump emphasized that after Iran's capitulation, the U.S. and allies would help make Iran 'economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before.'
He reiterated that there will be no deal with Iran except for 'unconditional surrender.'
Trump introduced a new slogan, 'MAKE IRAN GREAT AGAIN ,' paralleling his previous political branding.
title: Trump Says There Will Be No Deal with Iran Except 'Unconditional Surrender'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the BLS numbers as evidence of economic strain and policy failure—using phrases like "under strain," "destroyed," and "flashing red"—while right-leaning pieces stress how the miss complicates political standing and point to external causes, using words such as "sputtering," "dismal," or "jobs shock.

Pivotal dividing lines are attribution to presidential policy versus geopolitical/inflationary causes and whether one-off factors explain the drop; center outlets emphasize wage, participation and seasonality nuance de-emphasized by both sides, yet all note the surprise may alter Fed timing.
summary: On March 6, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the United States economy shed 92,000 jobs in February, and the unemployment rate rose to 4.4%.
Broader pressures such as tariffs, AI adoption, and high interest rates weighed on hiring, with construction losing 11,000 jobs last month and healthcare shedding 28,000 after a strike.

The Labor Department's revisions showed private payroll measures dipped by 86,000 in February, trimmed 69,000 jobs from December and January payroll revisions, and average hourly earnings rose 15 cents to $37.32.
Policymakers and markets reacted: the report could alter the Federal Reserve 's calculus ahead of its March 17 meeting as market futures fell and crude oil spiked amid the Iran conflict.
The report deepens a recent trend showing the three-month moving average fell to 6,000, with the breakeven hiring rate near zero to 50,000 jobs a month.
title: U.S. payrolls unexpectedly fell by 92,000 in February; unemployment rate rises to 4.4%

bias: Left coverage emphasizes authoritarian consolidation—using phrases like "cementing hardliners’ grip" and calling him "unqualified"—portraying the appointment as de facto hereditary succession; right coverage frames it as a security and legitimacy threat, foregrounding "US‑sanctioned," "Regime," "name not revealed" and ties to the Revolutionary Guards/IRGC and as defiance of U.S./Israeli pressure.
Center pieces de-emphasize partisanship, focusing on his low public profile and the Assembly of Experts and continuity.
The sharpest dividing lines are hereditary succession, IRGC links, and responses to the U.S./Israeli strikes; all agree state TV announced the 56‑year‑old son and that hardliners prevail.
summary: On Sunday, Iranian state TV announced that by a decisive vote, the Assembly of Experts appointed Ayatollah Seyyed Mojtaba Hosseini Khamenei as the new supreme leader.
Following the killing of Ali Khamenei on February 28, the 88-seat Assembly of Experts convened amid signs of a clerical rift and appointed Mojtaba as supreme leader on the ninth day of the war.

Mojtaba Khamenei , 56, is described as a mid-ranking cleric with influence inside Iran's security forces and vast business networks under Ali Khamenei, while the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps pledged its `sincere and life-long allegiance` and readiness to implement his orders.
Iran's IRGC will now report to the new supreme leader, who controls war strategy, and Israel warned it "will not hesitate to target you" and the IDF said it would "pursue every successor."
Seen as defiance against U.S. pressure, Iran's U.N. ambassador said at least 1332 Iranian civilians have been killed, amid Gulf strikes and U.S. demands for surrender.
title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report

bias: Left outlets emphasize state violence and humanitarian cost—foregrounding words like "kills," "strike," and qualifiers like "alleged" to cast doubt and frame the campaign as an escalating war with a mounting death toll .

Right outlets emphasize decisive law‑enforcement and military success, using triumphant language like "BOOM" and "blows up" and labels such as "narco‑terrorists" to criminalize targets and justify force.

Center coverage foregrounds legal and evidentiary questions—"legal quagmire," independent verification—while both sides echo SOUTHCOM’s account and the casualty count, revealing a shared national‑security framing amid ideological divides.
summary: On Sunday, the U.S. military said it killed six men in a strike on an alleged drug-smuggling vessel in the eastern Pacific Ocean and posted a video showing the boat exploding.

The campaign has so far killed at least 157 people since the Trump administration began in early September, while President Donald Trump calls it an 'armed conflict' with cartels despite little evidence provided.

U.S. Southern Command said it targeted alleged traffickers along known routes amid more than 40 known strikes in the Eastern Pacific Ocean and Caribbean Sea, but did not provide evidence the vessel carried drugs.
Critics noted the strikes' questionable legality and limited effectiveness, while Ecuador and the United States conducted operations this past week against organized crime groups.

With Saturday's gathering, Trump aimed to demonstrate that he remains committed to focusing U.S. foreign policy on the Western Hemisphere, even while waging a war on Iran, and encouraged Latin American leaders to join U.S. military action, citing cooperation with Ecuador this past week.
title: US military strikes another alleged drug boat in the Eastern Pacific, killing 6: SOUTHCOM

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the clash as escalation of a hate-driven rally, emphasizing a right‑wing organizer and terms like "rooted in bigotry and racism," while right-leaning outlets foreground suspects' faith and chants such as "Allahu Akbar" and use labels like "pro‑Islam agitators" and "IEDs" to portray an Islamist terror threat; center outlets de-emphasize attribution and stress verification, preferring "investigation/probe.
Pivotal dividing lines include organizer culpability, suspect religious ID, and whether coverage calls it "terror" versus a pending probe.
All sides agree an explosive device was thrown, arrests followed, and authorities are investigating—differences mirror wider ideological and cultural fault lines.
summary: During protests on Saturday, an ignited improvised explosive device was thrown outside the official residence of New York mayor Zohran Mamdani, and police said it was a viable device that could have caused serious injury or death, with Emir Balat and Ibrahim Kayumi arrested at the scene.
Police said the event tied to Jake Lang drew about 20 supporters and a much larger counter-protest of about 125 people, with two men from Pennsylvania angered by the anti-Islam demonstration.

Forensic descriptions show sports‑drink bottles inside glass jars with nuts and bolts and a hobby fuse; authorities said the fuse was linked to an M80‑type firework, and sources reported the devices contained TATP.
The FBI 's Joint Terrorism Task Force has taken the lead in a federal terrorism investigation, with search warrants expected in Pennsylvania and New Jersey and terrorism charges pending.
The mayor denounced the event as rooted in bigotry and racism, thanked police for quick action, and said `'Violence at a protest is never acceptable'` Sunday.
title: FBI Opens Terrorism Probe After IEDs Ignited Outside NYC Mayor's Home

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds escalation and human impact—highlighting families told to leave and framing an "ongoing conflict" tied to Iranian drone strikes—portraying the move as regional deterioration.
Right-Leaning coverage stresses authority and protection, using words like "orders," "nonessential," "precautionary measure" and an "Iran war" frame that emphasizes fear.

Pivotal dividing lines are whether the evacuation signals broader collapse versus prudent safeguarding and the emphasis on families versus institutional action; center adds timeline detail that left and right de-emphasize.
Both sides agree the State Department cited safety risks.
summary: On March 8, the U.S. State Department ordered American diplomats and U.S. embassy employees to leave Saudi Arabia , marking the first ordered departure since the Feb. 28 strikes.

After the Feb. 28 strikes, Iran's military launched barrages of missiles and drones targeting Gulf sites, with drones hitting the U.S. embassy in Riyadh and Saudi forces shooting down another over the diplomatic quarter.
Alison Dilworth, acting chief of mission, leads a mission without a Senate-confirmed ambassador as officials evacuated Americans on nearly two dozen charter flights, Dylan Johnson reported Sunday.

The move compels nonessential U.S. government personnel and families to depart, reducing diplomatic services as the State Department travel advisory urges reconsideration amid closed airspace and suspended commercial airlines.

Officials warned that further attacks are possible as Iran prepares for larger retaliation, while unrest in Pakistan left at least 22 dead and critics faulted the State Department for late warnings to thousands of American citizens in the region.
title: State Dept. Orders US Diplomats to Leave Saudi Arabia

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes institutional wrongdoing and victim narratives, using charged phrases like "biggest cover-up of my adult life," "mysterious device," and "directed energy weapon" and foregrounding foreign‑actor attribution and large case estimates ; right-leaning coverage instead foregrounds procurement mechanics and oversight, citing "secretly purchased"/"covert purchase," "mysterious ailments," and cautious tags such as "some investigators believe," de‑emphasizing attribution.

A key dividing line is blame—GRU attribution and cover‑up versus procurement secrecy—while center pieces stress the device "possibly linked" and that reporting "could finally solve" the mystery; all agree the government bought and tested the equipment and that investigative reporting raised serious concerns, reflecting deeper ideological and security priorities.
summary: 60 Minutes' new installment says the U.S. government acquired a directed-energy weapon from a Russian criminal network and has tested it on rats and sheep in a U.S. military laboratory for over a year.

Government-Sponsored expert panels led by Dr. David Relman concluded pulsed radiofrequency or microwave energy was plausibly linked to some cases, noting much research came from the former Soviet Union before a Nov. 18, 2025 meeting boosted support.
One victim, Catherine Werner, told 60 Minutes she woke up to 'intense pressure' and a 'low, humming sound,' followed by symptoms like vomiting and nosebleeds.

The Office of the Director of National Intelligence said a new review will be 'comprehensive and complete,' the Department of Defense declined to comment, and Dr. David Relman said parts of the government had 'And not only downplayed but dismissed, in some cases, buried.'

Investigators also reviewed over 1,000 classified documents as part of the inquiry, while the two exploratory NIH JAMA studies found no MRI-detectable brain injury and panels noted limited biological effect data.
title: 60 Minutes Reveals US Obtained Microwave Weapon Tied to Havana Syndrome

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame CENTCOM’s "safety warning" as a humanitarian advisory, briefly noting risk and urging civilians to "stay home" while de-emphasizing operational specifics; right-leaning coverage portrays Tehran as a culpable "regime," uses charged phrases like "blatantly disregards" and "heavily populated," highlights "one-way attack drones," named cities, released strike footage and U.S. casualties, and uniquely cites CENTCOM’s caveat that it cannot guarantee civilian safety near military-used sites.
Center outlets echo CENTCOM in institutional language and include the weapon/city allegations.
Shared ground: escalating operations and a common call for civilians to shelter.
summary: On Sunday, U.S. Central Command posted a safety notice on X shortly before 10 a.m. Eastern, urging Iranian civilians to stay indoors as Iranian forces launch drones and missiles from populated areas.

Following strikes that began Feb. 28, U.S. and Israeli forces targeted missile sites, military bases, and Tehran oil facilities, while Bahrain accused Iran of hitting a desalination/water-treatment plant.

Three named cities — Dezful, Esfahan and Shiraz — were identified where CENTCOM says military sites could lose protected status under international law, while U.S. military takes every feasible precaution to minimize harm to civilians.

At least 1,230 people have been killed in Iran, with losses in Lebanon, Israel and six U.S. troops; a late-night Israeli strike engulfed Tehran's oil facility in smoke, while Bahrain accused Iran of hitting a water treatment plant.
With interceptions reported in Qatar, Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, Adm. Brad Cooper said Iran is blatantly disregarding civilian lives while the conflict rattles global markets and disrupts air travel.
title: Centcom issues safety warning to Iranian civilians

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Tillis’s remarks as a damning judgment—emphasizing Miller’s "outsized influence," calling him "out of his depth" and an "embarrassment" and urging he "should go" with consequence-focused, pejorative language; right-leaning pieces pivot to defensive, identity-driven rhetoric, spotlighting social-media backlash, labeling Tillis a "RINO" or "washed-up" critic and praising Miller as a " MAGA " or "America First" warrior; centrist reports are more neutral, citing the "should leave" line without strong spin.
The key split is whether Miller is a governance liability or a loyal ideological standard-bearer, though all agree Tillis publicly said Miller should leave.
summary: On Sunday on CNN's State of the Union, Sen. Thom Tillis said Stephen Miller should leave, calling him `out of his depth` and a `big problem` for the administration.

Tillis argued Miller prioritizes political messaging over policy substance and exerts an `outsized influence` over Cabinet operations, limiting what qualified cabinet members can do and shaping the immigration crackdown.
Tillis said he was glad President Trump moved on from Kristi Noem and hopes Sen. Markwayne Mullin can `fix what's broken` at DHS, emphasizing he will be `independent` and `driven by data`.
Tillis warned that Miller `calling the shots` `gives me pause`, signaling pressure on the White House amid Mullin's expected confirmation, according to Sen. Thom Tillis.

Republican divisions surfaced after Sen. Lindsey Graham defended Stephen Miller last month, a longtime Trump adviser central to immigration enforcement, while Sen. Markwayne Mullin’s confirmation could trigger a special election under Oklahoma law.
title: Sen. Thom Tillis Says Stephen Miller Should Leave White House

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes sharp economic pain—using terms like "soars," "record high," and stressing oil above $100 a barrel—and treats official reassurances with skepticism, while right-leaning coverage downplays risk, using language such as "dismisses," "fiction," "short-term disruption," and ties the spike to policy success and operational details .
Rhetorically the left adopts an alarmed, critical tone and flags Trump's "ONLY FOOLS" remark; the right is reassuring and dismissive.
The center de-emphasized partisan framing, focusing on a "fear premium.
Both agree prices rose and officials called the rise temporary.
summary: Energy Secretary Chris Wright told CBS's Face the Nation on Sunday that "We have a temporary period of elevated energy prices, but it will not be long," adding the worst case is weeks, not months.
Escalation in the Strait of Hormuz has dramatically reduced shipments, with tanker transit near a standstill and only Iran-linked tankers crossing in the past 24 hours, heightening market concerns.

Available stockpiles and export capacity indicate the world is well supplied with no energy shortage in the Western Hemisphere, with over 400 million SPR barrels and refineries in Europe and Asia facing logistical interruptions alongside over 100 million barrels of Russia's floating crude.
The Trump administration announced a $20 billion reinsurance program and proposed U.S. Navy escorts, while Wright said the Strategic Petroleum Reserve could be used if needed.
Framing it as strategic, Wright argued the campaign will `bring in an era of even lower energy prices` once Iran is defanged, reducing threats to the U.S. and region.
title: Energy secretary says "period of elevated energy prices" will be temporary

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes human and economic fallout—using terms like "killed," "soar," and "launches more attacks" to frame U.S. culpability, highlight civilian harm and market turmoil while portraying succession as continuity of "hardline" rule; right-leaning pieces frame the choice as opaque and threatening, using "secretive," "biggest loser," and distrustful tones to stress regional rivalry and Tehran’s aggressiveness and de-emphasize casualty counts; center reports foreground institutional detail .
All sides nonetheless note the appointment, attacks and oil-price reaction, reflecting deeper geopolitical and ideological fault lines.
summary: On Monday, the Assembly of Experts named Mojtaba Khamenei supreme leader, elevating him to ayatollah shortly after midnight.
Amid conservative backing, Mojtaba Khamenei's appointment was framed as continuity, helped by ties to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and support from conservative factions in Iran.
The US in 2019 sanctioned him, noting he lacked an official post while representing his father, and opponents have accused him of involvement in the 2009 crackdown.
title: Succession defies Trump and raises stakes for wider showdown

bias: Left coverage frames the spike as politically damaging and impact-driven, using verbs like "soar," "impedes" and "skyrocketed" and stressing consumer pain and market fallout ; right coverage emphasizes security and agency, using "jump," "punches" and "Destruction," linking Strait of Hormuz closures and production cuts while highlighting official claims that effects are temporary; center foregrounds the president's "very small price to pay" quote with market context.

Pivotal divides include presidential trade‑off rhetoric, unverified military‑destruction claims, and official reassurances; both sides agree on the price milestone and supply disruptions, reflecting ideological splits over escalation versus economic impact.
summary: On Sunday, crude prices climbed above $100 after major Middle East producers cut output, with West Texas Intermediate at $101.56 and Brent crude at $101.81 amid Strait of Hormuz closure.
Following Iran's retaliatory strikes, ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has largely stopped, causing tanker operators to avoid the route and Gulf producers to cut output as storage fills.

Production from three main southern oilfields has plunged 70% to 1.3 million b/d, tightening markets, while Iraq's output has effectively collapsed and Kuwait Petroleum Corporation announced precautionary cuts Saturday.
U.S. crude surged about 35% last week, and U.S. pump prices rose to $3.45 on Sunday, prompting Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer to call for a reserve release.

Barclays and other analysts say shut-ins are spreading and could lift prices further; Goldman Sachs estimates only 4.2 million b/d can be rerouted, leaving roughly 16 million b/d potentially stranded if the passage is fully closed.
title: Oil prices top $100 per barrel as big Middle East producers cut output amid Iran war

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as a precautionary evacuation and later stresses officials’ finding the threat "not credible," highlighting restraint and institutional reassurance, while right-leaning pieces foreground alarm—using terms like "bomb threat," "swarm," "serious incident," and describing passengers as "confused and terrified"—to amplify danger and a law‑and‑order response.
Center outlets focus on timeline and official quotes, offering detail rather than tone.

Pivotal divides center on whether the credibility determination and passenger fear are emphasized; all sides agree on evacuation, FBI involvement and that the terminal reopened, reflecting security‑first versus caution‑and‑context priorities.
summary: Sunday afternoon, portions of Kansas City International Airport were evacuated after a reported threat around 11:15 a.m., airport spokesperson Jackson Overstreet said, and the terminal reopened later that day.
After the alert, federal and city partners moved to substantiate whether the threat was credible as Airport Police worked with the FBI and conducted parking garage searches.
Agents with K-9 units opened gates and advised roughly 2,000 passengers to line up between Terminal A and Terminal B around 12:05 p.m., video shows travelers evacuating onto the tarmac.

FBI Director Kash Patel said the FBI reviewed the threat and determined it was not credible, and U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy posted that the incident `is now clear and normal operations are resuming`.
Flights to and from Kansas City Airport were grounded and traffic diverted during the incident, echoing a Dec. 31, 2025 evacuation that also ended with no credible threat.
title: Passengers evacuated at KCI after possible threat; FBI investigating

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as partisan conflict and escalation risk—using phrases like "at odds," "risk," and "dwindling"—to underscore skepticism of administration assurances; right-leaning pieces foreground national-security urgency, invoking " military aggression," "worried," and highlighting defense contractors ramping up production.

The left’s cautionary, adversarial tone contrasts with the right’s securitized, urgent rhetoric; pivotal divides center on the Pentagon claim it "has everything it needs" versus a "long-standing production problem" and replenishment timelines .
Both sides agree strikes raise supply concerns and note production shortfalls, reflecting ideological and geopolitical priorities.
summary: The U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran have raised concerns among Democrats about diminishing American stockpiles of certain weapons due to a long-standing production problem.
Some experts say the dwindling stockpiles could present challenges if another conflict emerges after the Iran war, as interceptors were heavily used up defending Israel.
The Trump administration has promised to boost defense spending, speed up production, and call out underperforming contractors to address the issue.
title: Trump administration and Democrats at odds over risk to US weapons stockpiles from Iran war

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump's remark as an aggressive, escalation-prone threat—using terms like "threatens," "plunged the Middle East into war," and treating "won't last long" as coercive and emphasizing human cost—while right-leaning coverage portrays it as decisive leadership, valorizing "final say/approval from us," "warns," and ideas like "unconditional surrender" to justify firmness.
Center outlets mainly quote the line and note Iran's insistence on sovereignty, which the left de-emphasizes.
The pivotal divide is whether the comment signals necessary deterrence or dangerous overreach; both sides agree a contested succession and regional tensions are underway.
summary: On Sunday, US President Donald Trump warned Iran's next supreme leader would not `last long` without US approval, saying `He's going to have to get approval from us` as Iran's clerical body may soon announce a new leader.
Facing meeting constraints, the Assembly of Experts, the 88-member clerical body, said it reached a majority consensus, though some procedural obstacles remain, as announced by Ayatollah Mirbaqeri.
The United States has not ruled out military options, including sending special forces to secure enriched uranium, while Trump said he seeks a permanent nuclear solution.
Israel's military warned it would pursue and target members of Iran's Assembly of Experts who appoint Mojtaba Khamenei as supreme leader, amid threats to clerics.
Iranian officials rejected outside interference and Israel's widescale strikes on Tehran hit at least four oil depots and a logistics site, deepening regional instability.
title: Iran's next supreme leader won't 'last long' without my approval, Trump says

bias: Left outlets frame the diversion as a precautionary misunderstanding—emphasizing passengers' fear, video cues like "Heads Down! Hands Up!" and phrases such as "out of an abundance of caution" and "no credible threat"—while right outlets amplify alarm with "bomb threat," "guns drawn," "dragged off"/"raiding" language and link the episode to DHS funding shortfalls and geopolitical "panic.
Center outlets tend to foreground official sourcing but sometimes spotlight "chaos" or policy calls.

Pivotal divides are the label "bomb threat" versus "misunderstanding" and the portrayal of force; all sides agree on the diversion, removal and the FBI 's clearance, but interpret those facts through law‑and‑order versus civil‑liberties prisms.
summary: On Friday, March 6, Southwest Airlines Flight 2094 diverted to ATL after crew reported a possible security matter, landing at 9:06 p.m. local time.

After the crew reported the issue, aviation industry security protocol required landing and turning the disturbance over to law enforcement on the ground, prompting the diversion and removal of a passenger.

Passenger video shows passengers ordered to keep their heads down as four police officers pulled an unidentified man, with a TikTok clip shared more than 1.8 million times; the 'Bomb threat' caption remains uncorroborated.

FBI agents and Atlanta Police Department interviewed the person removed and later determined there was no credible threat; officials said no charges will be filed, and Southwest Airlines re-accommodated passengers who reached Fort Lauderdale just before 3:30 a.m.

The Atlanta Police Department said `Atlanta Police assisted our federal partners with last night's incident at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport`, while weather had rerouted seven flights and caused delays.
title: FL-Bound Flight Diverts To Atlanta Over 'Security Matter'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Newsom as pressing for aid—highlighting "fire victims," deriding Noem with "Kosplay Kristi," calling the $220M expendature "ad money" and amplifying a claim that $500M is "stalled" and should be released; right-leaning coverage is combative, using "rips," "Kosplay Barbie," and foregrounding the $500M as an urgent FEMA/DHS release while criticizing political spending; center coverage is more restrained, "calls on" DHS and focuses on redirecting roughly $220M.

Pivotal dividing lines are the $500M/"stalled" claim and DHS confirmation, yet all agree Newsom urged redirecting ad-related funds toward LA wildfire recovery—reflecting deeper debates over redistributive aid versus fiscal/administrative accountability.
summary: California Governor Gavin Newsom accused Department of Homeland Security head Kristi Noem of withholding over $500 million in FEMA wildfire recovery funds for Los Angeles by not signing contracts to release the money.
Newsom condemned a $220 million DHS ad campaign featuring Noem on horseback and demanded unused funds be redirected to help wildfire survivors rebuild.

Noem was removed from her DHS position and reassigned as Special Envoy for the Shield of the Americas amid criticism over funding delays and management policies requiring her personal approval of contracts over $100,000.
Critics said Noem's approval policy delayed FEMA funding needed for rebuilding roads, schools, and disaster prevention in Los Angeles.
title: Newsom calls on DHS to direct Noem ad campaign funding to Los Angeles fire recovery
Trump praises Italy PM Meloni's willingness to help in U.S.-Israel war with Iran

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the president’s attribution as a deflection, highlighting investigators and graphic suffering with charged terms like "blown to pieces" and calls it a "BIG LIE," while right-leaning pieces foreground the leader’s claim , stress Iranian munitions’ alleged inaccuracy with phrases such as "They have no accuracy whatsoever," and amplify decisive tones—"insists," "Will Not Hit," even upbeat economics like "below $3 soon.
Pivotal divides center on attribution vs. investigative forensics and vivid victim detail; center outlets present the claim as contested with caveats.
Both sides agree a deadly strike occurred and officials are investigating.
summary: Chris Wright, U.S. Energy Secretary, said on Sunday the United States will spare Iran's energy infrastructure with no plans to target its oil or gas industry.

Israel attacked oil storage facilities in and around Tehran on March 7, sparking huge fires, while the war has all but shut down the Strait of Hormuz, affecting nearly 20 percent of crude and LNG transit.
Fuel-Price trackers reported sizable weekly gains as West Texas Intermediate rose 12 percent on Friday and was up 36 percent in a week, AAA said.

Washington is arranging protection for shipping, with US officials talking to shipping companies about moving vessels out of the Gulf while early tankers likely receive US military protection and a $20 billion reinsurance plan covers Strait of Hormuz transit risk.

The Energy Secretary downplayed lasting damage by saying disruptions to the petroleum and gas industry will be short lived, and Wright told CBS, `They shouldn't go much higher than they are here because the world is very well supplied with oil`.
title: Trump, Hegseth claim Iran bombed girls’ school, not the US

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s ultimatum as escalation—using charged labels like "voter suppression," "desperate," and "tirade"—and stresses how proof-of-citizenship and photo‑ID rules could disenfranchise vulnerable voters; right-leaning coverage frames those measures as "election integrity," praises his firmness with terms like "vows" and "MUST GO TO THE FRONT OF THE LINE," and foregrounds filibuster workarounds.
Center outlets emphasize filibuster hurdles and "escalating pressure.
Pivotal divides include intent , tactics , and demographic impact ; all sides note the House passage, Trump’s post, and the high-stakes political stakes.
summary: On Mar 8, President Donald Trump vowed on Truth Social he would not sign any bills until Congress passes the SAVE America Act, urging Republicans to bypass the filibuster.
The House of Representatives passed the SAVE America Act last month; it stalls in the U.S. Senate where Republicans hold 53 seats and would require proof of citizenship and photo ID to vote.
Demanding additions, Trump pushed strict voter-ID and proof-of-citizenship rules, limits on mail-in ballots with exceptions for military and voters with illness or disability.

Senate Democrats say they will oppose the SAVE Act and warned Trump's signature blockade could imperil must-pass Department of Homeland Security funding, with Congress able to counter using veto, pocket veto, and 10-day enactment rules.

With midterms looming, Democratic Party leaders say the SAVE Act would suppress votes for college students, senior citizens, Americans living abroad, and adoptees; an NBC News poll found 62% disapprove of Trump's handling of inflation.
title: Trump says he won’t sign any bills into law until SAVE Act passes

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as a threat to progressive leadership and far‑right provocation, using words like "suspicious," "far‑right" and "Islamophobic" and amplifying official language "IED" and "serious injury or death" to stress targeted danger; right‑leaning coverage emphasizes law‑and‑order and a "terror scare," highlighting "homemade explosives," "hurled," "NYPD tackling," and "horrifying/terrifying" tones to justify security focus and sometimes assert the mayor was home.

Pivotal divides include whether attackers' motives or identities are foregrounded, inconsistent arrest counts and mayor‑presence claims; nonetheless all cite bomb‑squad findings, device composition, arrests and FBI interest.
summary: On Saturday, March 07, 2026, two small `suspicious devices` were lit and thrown near Gracie Mansion, and the NYPD Bomb Squad recovered and tested them while two detained individuals were taken into custody.
Jake Lang organized an anti-Muslim demonstration outside Gracie Mansion, where about 20 Lang-associated demonstrators faced roughly 125 counterprotesters, raising tensions.

Police said the devices appeared to be jars wrapped in black tape with nuts, bolts, screws, and a hobby fuse; Emir Balat, 18-year-old counterprotester, ignited and threw one device, which hit a barrier and extinguished.
Authorities conducted K-9 sweeps and called in an FBI terrorism task force, while Mamdani and his wife remained safe, and officials said testing is underway to classify the devices.
Joe Calvello condemned the rally as `despicable and Islamaphobic`, highlighting threats to officials and residents, with Lang called a `vile white supremacist`.
title: Man Probed After ‘Improvised Explosive Device’ Thrown Near Mamdani’s Residence Amid Anti-Muslim Protest

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the events as state aggression and dynastic consolidation, emphasizing verbs and phrases like "fires," "new," and actions "in the name of" the new leader to underscore political symbolism and regional targeting , while right-leaning pieces foreground immediate military threat and a hawkish security frame, using terms like "takes charge" and emphasizing retaliation.
Centers add causal timeline—"nine days after" US‑Israeli strikes—and terms such as "slain/killed/late" and "fresh missile barrage.
Pivotal divides are causal timing and Gulf targets, yet all sides report missile strikes, the new leader’s selection, and prior threats, reflecting deeper geopolitical and ideological rifts.
summary: On Monday, Iran fired missiles at Israel invoking Mojtaba Khamenei , with blasts heard over Tel Aviv and six people wounded, while explosions were reported in Qatar.

The Assembly of Experts convened nine days after the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei to appoint Mojtaba Khamenei as the third supreme leader, reading the decision on state television despite what it called "the brutal aggression of the criminal America and the evil Zionist regime."

Israel's strikes on Tehran hit five oil facilities early Sunday, killing at least four and darkening the capital's air for 10 million, while benchmark crude price soared beyond $100 and markets in Japan and South Korea opened sharply lower on Monday.
Iran's health ministry reported at least 1,200 civilians killed and 10,000 wounded, while Bahrain, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia suffered infrastructure damage and casualties in Al Kharj.
The Revolutionary Guards signalled capacity for a sustained campaign lasting up to six months and pledged obedience to Mojtaba, while Israel warned any successor would be targeted.
title: Iran fires new missiles as Khamenei's son takes charge

bias: Left-Leaning narratives frame the Iranian president’s apology as evidence of internal "fracturing," highlighting a divide between "pragmatic" and "hardline" factions.
In contrast, right-leaning outlets portray the gesture as "humiliating" and "degrading," emphasizing nationalist demands to "take the microphone away.
While the left focuses on diplomatic shifts, the right prioritizes perceived regime weakness, even suggesting the president apologize domestically.
Center-Leaning coverage employs sensationalist verbs like "slammed" and "pillory" while including broader regional context.
Both perspectives agree the apology was "weak" and "unacceptable" to critics, illustrating deep ideological divides over international conciliation versus national strength.
summary: On Saturday, March 7, 2026, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologised on state media, saying `I personally apologize to neighbouring countries that were affected by Iran 's actions`, but omitted the apology on social media.
Amid intense external strikes, the interim council including Pezeshkian agreed to suspend attacks on nearby states, prompting de-escalation gestures amid a leadership vacuum after Khamenei's death.
Hardline cleric Hamid Rasai called Pezeshkian's stance `unprofessional, weak and unacceptable`, and Raja News described it as `begging diplomacy`, urging to `take the microphone away` from him.

The episode has revealed visible fractures within Iran's ruling elite as hardliners, including judiciary chief Ayatollah Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, pushed retaliatory strikes, and senior ayatollahs urged the Assembly of Experts to speed leadership selection.

Meanwhile, the regional war has spilled into neighbouring states and markets as U.S.-Israeli strikes killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians, Iranian attacks killed 10 in Israel and six U.S. service members, and Gulf output cuts disrupted flights.
title: Israel, Iran trade more blows, Iranian president draws criticism at home for apology

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames detainees as "pro‑Palestinian activists" organizing a "humanitarian flotilla" and emphasizes that authorities "detained" them—conveying solidarity and rights-focused concern—while right-leaning pieces de-emphasize that portrayal, calling the mission a "phantom humanitarian mission" and flagging "irregularities," adopting a skeptical, accusatory tone.
Left uses legitimizing labels and empathetic diction; right uses delegitimizing, judgmental language.
Pivotal dividing lines are the flotilla's legitimacy, organizers' stated aims, and the reported financial inquiry.
Center reports use neutral verbs and affiliation labels.
Both sides agree detentions occurred and tensions mounted, reflecting deeper ideological splits over activism versus security.
summary: On Friday, Tunisian authorities detained several pro-Palestinian activists amid a probe by Tunisia 's National Guard financial crimes unit into donation funds for a planned flotilla.
Authorities banned a planned event Thursday in Tunis expected to host Thiago Ávila, Greta Thunberg and Seif Abu Koshk, while earlier this week security forces blocked a ceremony at Sidi Bou Said.
Organizers say the new flotilla will include more than 1,000 activists and sail from Spain, Tunisia and Italy with a land convoy expected to attract thousands more.

Global Sumud Flotilla organizers accused authorities of targeting activists and called for their immediate release; Tunisian authorities did not comment, and Ávila was detained Friday at Brussels Airport.

The move occurs amid Gaza's ongoing humanitarian crisis as Israel suspended more than two dozen humanitarian organizations and the United Nations reports large aid stockpiles since the October ceasefire.
title: Tunisian authorities detain pro-Palestinian activists preparing new Gaza aid flotilla

bias: Left coverage emphasizes legality and moral alarm—highlighting Pedro Sánchez ’s "extraordinary mistake" and warning of costly consequences—while right coverage frames the strikes as strategic opportunity, portraying leaders as actively "reshaping the Mideast" and citing rhetoric that Iran has "authorized to shoot as they see fit," calling the move a "gamble.
A key dividing line is debates over legality and leader agency versus interpretations of permissive Iranian rhetoric; tactical specifics are center-focused and de-emphasized by left/right.
Both sides nonetheless note escalation, spillover risks, and political costs for Trump.
summary: One week into the US-Israeli war, President Donald Trump faces a growing list of risks that raise doubts about translating battlefield gains into a geopolitical win.

Trump signalled wider aims including regime change, demanding `unconditional surrender` on March 6, told Reuters on March 5 he would pick Iran's next leader, urged Iranian Kurdish rebels, and pressed strikes despite senior aides' warnings.
American casualties remain low with six service members killed, and Iran has activated proxies as Lebanon's Hezbollah renewed hostilities while tanker traffic halted in the Strait of Hormuz.
title: One Week Into Iran War, the Dangers for the U.S. and Trump Multiply

bias: Right-Leaning coverage frames Pahlavi as a legitimized, proactive "exiled" claimant—using language like "crumbling," "teetering," and "accepted responsibility" to cast the Islamic Republic as imploding and to tout his role as a "transitional leader," often highlighting his condemnation of Iranian strikes as "unacceptable violations of sovereignty" and ties to U.S. support; center coverage is more conditional, emphasizing he is "ready to lead" and willing to "work with the West" without dramatic collapse claims; left-leaning coverage is de-emphasized.
Both sides note Pahlavi’s claim to a mandate and the pivotal moment tied to Khamenei’s death, revealing ideological splits over intervention, legitimacy and alignment.
summary: Reza Pahlavi, exiled crown prince and son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, said he is ready to lead Iran if the current government collapses after many Iranians asked him to guide a transition.

With no successor announced, Iran is currently governed by a temporary leadership council, following operations last weekend that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, which he said was caused by operations resulting in his death.

Pahlavi argued, calling for rebuilding ties with Western countries and Israel and urging Arab states to support a future transitional government, emphasizing only the ballot box should determine Iran's future.
Roughly, the domestic picture shows growing street readiness and elements of the regime are 'crumbling,' Pahlavi said he aims to restore stability and let Iranians decide through elections.

Looking to the region and economy, he argues that a democratic Iran would boost regional stability and open large U.S. economic opportunities, including more than $1 trillion in revenue over a decade.
title: Exiled Iranian crown prince says regime is 'crumbling' after killing of Khamenei, other leaders

bias: Left outlets frame Obama’s eulogy as a principled defense—using terms like "blistering rebuke," praise such as "beautiful," and "swiping," emphasizing threats to "assaults on democratic institutions" and Jackson's legacy; right outlets portray the same remarks as opportunistic and disrespectful, deploying hyperbole—phrases like "absolutely disgusting attack," "Disrespect on Steroids," "the Worst People," all‑caps "FIRE," and "divider‑in‑chief"—and amplify White House pushback, which left de‑emphasized while right de‑emphasized civil‑rights praise.
Key divides include whether the funeral was politicized and Obama’s refusal to name the president; center attributes claims neutrally.
Both agree on the event and Obama’s critique, reflecting deeper ideological fault lines.
summary: Barack Obama warned the U.S. faces “daily assaults on democracy” while speaking at a memorial for civil rights leader Jesse Jackson.
The event in Chicago was attended by former leaders including Joe Biden, Bill Clinton, and former Vice President Kamala Harris.
Donald Trump did not attend the memorial due to scheduling, according to a White House official.
title: US faces assaults on democracy, Obama says at Jesse Jackson memorial

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the joint Pentagon– FAA laser trials as militarization and disruption—emphasizing phrases like "suddenly close airspace" and spotlighting deployments—while right-leaning pieces present the tests as necessary security measures, using terms such as "anti-drone," "will conduct," and calling the target "seemingly threatening.
Center outlets de-emphasize partisanship and stress technical integration and "cutting-edge" safety partnerships.

Pivotal flashpoints splitting reactions include the airspace closures, labeling the lasers as "shoot down" systems, and limited legal/regulatory discussion; all agree the tests address FAA safety concerns after recent incidents, reflecting a security versus civil‑liberties divide.
summary: On March 7-8, the Department of Defense and the Federal Aviation Administration will test a high-energy laser system at White Sands Missile Range, New Mexico, after recent FAA airspace closures in Texas.

Because the FAA abruptly closed airspace after early-February incidents, the Department of Defense mistakenly shot down a CBP drone near Fort Hancock, Texas, on Feb. 25, and a Feb. 26 laser use, lawmakers said, led to another drone shootdown.

Testing will gather data on aircrew eye safety and aircraft surrogate models while validating automated safety shut-off systems, and the laser will engage targets attended by multiple agencies including the Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, Department of Homeland Security, and New Mexico National Guard.

The White Sands tests are intended as a first step toward establishing a formal coordination framework for future laser deployments, the Pentagon said they underscore a unified federal effort to counter unmanned aerial threats while maintaining national airspace safety and sovereignty.
Longer term, the U.S. Army's RFI for up to 20 lasers and JIATF-401's marketplace of over 1,600 anti-drone products reflect procurement growth amid legal FAA notification rules.
title: Pentagon and FAA agree to conduct anti-drone laser tests in New Mexico

bias: Coverage splits sharply: right-leaning pieces foreground individual liberty—using phrasing like "your boss can't force you" and evocative terms such as "microchipped" to cast the law as a safeguard against coercion—while left-leaning reporting oscillates between framing employers as intrusive and mocking regulatory overreach with phrases like "fun police" or sympathizing with "innocent bosses.

Center pieces are more procedural, citing HB2303 and its delivery to the governor's desk and focusing on legal text; all sides nonetheless emphasize the bill’s ban on forced implants, exposing a clash over autonomy versus regulatory reach.
summary: Wednesday's unanimous Senate approval sent House Bill 2303 to Governor Bob Ferguson for final signing.
Introduced earlier this year by Rep. Brianna Thomas and Rep. Lisa Parshley, the measure passed the House 86-6 on Feb. 14, advancing to the Senate for approval.
The bill defines subcutaneous microchips and excludes adhesive wearables, and sets a $10,000 minimum fine for first offense, as HB 2303 specifies.
If Gov. Bob Ferguson signs the bill, Washington would become the 14th state to preemptively ban mandatory workplace microchipping, allowing workers to sue for damages and attorney fees.
Past voluntary examples include Three Square Market in 2017 and Biohax's 4,000-chip rollout in 2018, with lawmakers noting no known U.S. cases of employer-mandated microchipping.
title: Washington Passes Ban on Mandatory Workplace Microchips

summary: On Saturday, President Donald Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One that he had told Kurdish forces not to enter the Iran war after attending a dignified transfer for six U.S. service members.

Trump said he ruled out Kurdish participation because he didn’t want to make the war more complex and opposed their involvement to prevent harm despite their willingness, while Israel’s military pushed for Kurdish action and CNN reported the CIA sought to arm Kurdish forces.

Trump reinforced his stance with a warning, saying `I would say, if we ever did that, they would be so decimated that they wouldn't be able to fight at the ground level`, as U.S. and Israeli strikes continued and about 10,000 civilian structures were damaged, the Iranian Red Crescent reported.
As a result, immediate implications include Trump not ruling out U.S. ground troops while vowing to minimize troop deaths as Iran's president threatens attacks.

Regional reactions underscore that Turkey on March 5 warned PJAK activity threatens stability, airstrikes hit Iranian forces near Erbil, and Human Rights Watch called for a war-crime probe after a deadly school attack.
title: Trump says he's ruled out having Kurds join Iran war

bias: Left outlets frame the story as AI-driven expansion “spurring” big, systemic risks—even “threatening to overwhelm the power grid”—and emphasize community harm, while right outlets frame homeowners as embattled defenders, “fighting back,” stressing private-property loss and rural burdens; left uses alarmist scale words and national-security framing, right uses populist, emotive anecdotes to elicit sympathy.
Centers de-emphasize national-security rhetoric, focusing on policy and planning trade-offs.
Both sides note AI demand, long transmission routes and multibillion-dollar costs , revealing ideological splits over community vs. growth priorities.
summary: The rapid growth of AI data centers is increasing power needs in the U.S., pushing utilities to expand the electric grid.
Power companies are proposing new high-voltage transmission lines across long distances, costing tens of billions of dollars, to move electricity and maintain grid reliability.

Landowners, local communities, and environmental groups are challenging these transmission projects due to concerns about property impacts, environmental damage, and who benefits from the infrastructure.
title: Rapid Expansion of High-Voltage Power Lines Meets Local Opposition Across US

bias: Left outlets frame the 61st anniversary as urgent, using words like "violence," "worries," "dire" and "calls to action" and highlighting organizers and local details , while right coverage emphasizes commemoration—"commemorates," "historic"—and a reverent, remembrance tone that de-emphasizes legal specifics; center pieces stress a pending Supreme Court case and sober "worries/concerns.

The pivotal dividing lines are whether the event is a mobilizing crisis or chiefly a historical remembrance and how prominently the court challenge is featured; all sides nonetheless note the 1965 violence shocked the nation, spurred the Voting Rights Act, and that thousands gathered.
summary: Thousands have gathered in Selma this weekend, and the commemorative march across the Edmund Pettus Bridge is set to conclude the anniversary events, with legal stakes looming nearby.
Long ago, the March 7, 1965 violence known as Bloody Sunday shocked the nation and helped spur the Voting Rights Act's passage.
Charles Mauldin, 78, warned that past advances risk being eradicated and recalled brutalization by state authorities during the 1965 march.

The U.S. Supreme Court is expected to rule soon on a Louisiana case about the role of race in drawing districts, with a limiting ruling possibly enabling Republican-controlled states to redraw and roll back majority Black and Latino districts, as seen in Rep. Shomari Figures' 2024 win.
At the apex of the bridge, marchers encountered a large law enforcement presence, including officers on horseback, while organizers urged attendees to keep pressing forward like in 1965.
title: Thousands Mark Selma March Anniversary Amid Voting Rights Act Case
